mūla.devanāgarī: ज्ञान-यज्ञेन चाप्य् अन्ये यजन्तो माम् उपासते | एकत्वेन पृथक्त्वेन बहुधा विश्वतो-मुखम्
mūla.iast: jñāna-yajñena cāpy anye yajanto mām upāsate | ekatvena pṛthaktvena bahudhā viśvato-mukham
mūla.chapter_position: Chapter 9 (Rāja-Vidyā-Rāja-Guhya-Yoga (The Yoga of Royal Knowledge and Royal Mystery)), verse 15
mūla.speaker: Krishna

senses_attested_in_panel.sense: च यजन्तः माम् उपासते? कथम् बहुधा पृथक्त्वेन जगदाकारेण विश्वतोमुखं विश्वप्रकारम् अवस्थितं माम् एकत्वेन उपासते
अपि महात्मानः पूर्वोक्तैः कीर्तनादिभिः ज्ञानाख्येन यज्ञेन
पूर्वोक्तसाधनानुष्ठानासमर्थाः ज्ञानयज्ञेनत्वं वा अहमस्मि भगवो देवते अहं वै त्वमसि इत्यादिश्रुत्युक्तमहग्रहापासनं ज्ञानं

word_by_word.grammar: 3rd person singular present indicative active verb

senses_attested_in_panel.sense: पृथक्त्वेन जगदाकारेण विश्वतोमुखं विश्वप्रकारम् अवस्थितं माम् एकत्वेन उपासते
हि तस्य रूपं,आभाति शुक्लमिव लोहितमिवाथो नीलमथोऽर्जुनं इति सनत्सुजाते [म.भा.5।44।26]दैवमेवापरे [4।25] इत्युक्तप्रकारेण ब
तैस्तैर्बहुभिः प्रकारैर्विश्वरूपं सर्वात्मानं मामेवोपासते तेन तेन ज्ञानयज्ञेनेति उत्तरोत्तराणां क्रमेण पूर्वपूर्वभूमिला

corpus_provenance.mūla: Belvalkar critical edition (BORI 1947), via Ambuda multi-witness

so_what_questions: If all three modes — ekatva, pṛthaktva, bahudhā — are valid worship of the same Kṛṣṇa, what does this imply about the claims of any single darśana (school of thought) to exclusive soteriological truth?

Madhusūdana proposes a three-tier hierarchy (uttama / madhyama / manda): does this hierarchy help practitioners locate themselves honestly, or does it risk entrenching spiritual elitism within a tradition that elsewhere insists all paths lead to the same summit?
Jñāna-yajña is described as offering knowledge itself as the sacrifice — in what sense does intellectual inquiry, when directed toward the divine, become a ritual act rather than a cognitive one?

Rāmānuja insists that worshipping cosmic diversity IS worshipping Bhagavān because the world is His body (śarīra); does this ontology dissolve the boundary between ecological reverence and theistic devotion?

Madhva's pṛthaktva holds that the absolute distinction of Nārāyaṇa from all other beings is itself the highest truth to be worshipped — how does a theology of radical otherness generate intimacy rather than distance?
Vallabha includes Śiva, Śakti, Sūrya, and Gaṇeśa as valid forms of the one Kṛṣṇa — what are the implications for inter-sectarian conflict within Hinduism if this verse is taken as authoritative?

The verse presents ekatvena, pṛthaktvena, and bahudhā as simultaneous, coexisting modes of worship rather than sequential stages — is this an argument for religious pluralism, or is it a claim that all apparent diversity is already contained within one framework?

advaita.application: Before any focused intellectual work — reading, research, problem-solving — pause and recognize that the attention you are bringing is itself offered to the one Consciousness that underlies both knower and known. This is jñāna-yajña in daily life: treating the act of clear seeing (paramārtha-darśana) as a sacred offering rather than a personal achievement. When diverse views or people frustrate you, attempt ekatvena — ask whether the friction reveals your forgetting that one Brahman appears in all these forms.

viśiṣṭādvaita.application: When you tend to another person's needs — cooking for family, caring for the ill, maintaining shared spaces — recognize that you are serving Bhagavān's own body (śarīra), since all cit and acit are His. This makes service (kainkarya) both cosmically grounded and radically equalizing: the difficult colleague, the demanding child, and the broken infrastructure are all sites of the same divine embodiment. Pṛthaktva-worship becomes gratitude for the rich specificity of that body.

dvaita.application: Practice the discipline of pṛthaktva (distinction) in your own self-understanding: resist the habit of inflating your own capacities into absolute claims. Nārāyaṇa's vailakṣaṇya (sovereign uniqueness) is the measure by which your own dependence becomes clear — not as humiliation but as structural honesty. Daily prayer in the Dvaita mode is less about dissolving self than about accurately naming the asymmetry: the jīva is real, good, and precious — and wholly dependent. That clarity is itself a form of worship.

śuddhādvaita.application: Within Puṣṭi-mārga, the application is receptivity rather than effort: allow Kṛṣṇa's grace (puṣṭi) to determine the mode of your worship rather than choosing a mode strategically. If today's energy is dāso'smi (differentiated devotion) and tomorrow's is identity-absorption, neither is a failure. The practical instruction is to recognize that even informal, scattered devotion — humming a bhajan while working, noticing beauty in small things — is already bahudhā worship of the cosmic-faced one. No posture is too undignified for the Lord's own presence to inhabit.

bhakti.application: The Śrīdhara application is recognition: in any encounter with another mind — conversation, reading, encounter with nature — practice seeing vāsudevaḥ sarvam (Vāsudeva is everything) as a living perception, not a doctrinal formula. When you notice the impulse to categorize a person as lesser or irrelevant, the countermovement is sarvātmatva-darśana — seeing them as a face of the all-faced one. The shift is not sentimental but perceptual: it changes what information you attend to and what questions you ask.

advaita-bhakti.application: Madhusūdana's graduated ladder offers self-assessment without self-rejection: locate honestly which tier describes your current practice — are you capable of sustained identity-contemplation (uttama), or do you need a concrete divine image or proximate symbol (madhyama), or are you currently navigating life through action and diverse practices (manda)? Then practice at that tier fully rather than pretending to a higher one. The ladder guarantees upward movement (pūrva-bhūmi-lābha) only if you occupy your actual rung; spiritual inflation blocks the climb.
